        print(f"color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), children={n['children']}")

Wait, but earlier when I absorbed the charcoal at (36,32), the blue bar shrank from 28 to 18 pixels. And now the blue bar is 28 pixels again. So absorbing a charcoal square shrinks the blue bar, and moving the white strip expands it back.
So the goal might be to absorb all charcoal squares and then the blue bar shrinks permanently. But moving the white strip expands the blue bar back.
Actually, let me reconsider. The blue bar at (28,28)-(31,39) is 12 columns wide. The white strip at (28,24)-(32,27) is 4 columns wide. They're adjacent.
I think the blue bar represents the "unabsorbed" portion of the level, and the white strip represents the "absorbed" portion. When I absorb a charcoal square, the white strip grows and the blue bar shrinks. When I move the white strip, the blue bar expands to fill the vacated space.

Actually, the absorption mechanic requires the white strip to be below the charcoal. But the white strip is now 5x4, not 4x1. So the "below" relationship is different.
